title: Instance Attributes 🏷️
description: Give your objects permanent data using Instance Variables.

slides.title: State (Data) 🧠
slides.content:
Right now, our `Dog` objects are totally blank. They have no data!

Every object in the real world has characteristics. A dog has a name, a breed, and an age. 

In programming, we call these characteristics **State**.

slides.title: Instance Variables 🏷️
slides.content:
Remember how normal variables vanish when a method finishes?

To make data stick permanently to an object, you must use a special variable called an **Instance Variable**.

Instance Variables always start with the `@` symbol.

slides.title: Setting State
slides.content: Because we used the `@` symbol, the `name` data is permanently glued to the object inside its memory block, not just the method!
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  def set_name
    @name = "Fido"
  end
  
  def speak
    puts "Woof! I am #{@name}!"
  end
end

my_dog = Dog.new
my_dog.set_name
my_dog.speak

quizData.question: What symbol must be placed at the very front of a variable name to define it as an Instance Variable attached to the Object?
quizData.explanation: Variables starting with `@` (like `@name`) are scoped to the entire Object itself, not just the tiny method they are inside of.

slides.title: Every Object is Unique ✨
slides.content:
Remember, a Class is a blueprint. We can build 100 dogs from it.

Because Instance Variables (`@name`) are glued to the *Object* (the instance), each of our 100 dogs can have a completely different name without interfering with the others!

slides.title: Multiple Objects in Memory
slides.content: Run this! See how we used the exact same Class blueprint, but the two actual Objects hold totally unique data?
slides.codeSnippet:
class Dog
  def set_name(name_text)
    @name = name_text
  end
  
  def show_name
    puts "My name is #{@name}!"
  end
end

dog1 = Dog.new
dog1.set_name("Fido")

dog2 = Dog.new
dog2.set_name("Buddy")

dog1.show_name
dog2.show_name

quizData.question: If you build two Objects from the same Class blueprint, do their Instance Variables share data?

options.text: No, each Object is a completely unique instance holding its own private data.
Yes, if you change `@name` on Dog 1, it changes on Dog 2 as well.
Yes, but only if they are the same breed.

quizData.explanation: Every time you call `.new`, a fresh walled garden of memory is created. They do not share data.
